expanded_description:
The IS-LM model, developed by John Hicks (1937) and Alvin Hansen (1949), is a macroeconomic framework that shows the relationship between interest rates and real output in the goods market (IS curve) and the money market (LM curve). It was the dominant macroeconomic model from the 1950s through the 1970s and remains a foundational tool for understanding the interaction between real and financial sectors, the effects of monetary and fiscal policy, and the determination of short-run equilibrium output and interest rates.

The IS-LM model is built on several key assumptions that define its scope and applicability. These assumptions are critical for understanding what the model can and cannot explain, and for interpreting its predictions correctly. The three core assumptions are: (1) fixed prices (the price level is assumed constant in the short run), (2) a closed economy (no international trade or capital flows), and (3) a short-run framework (capital stock and technology are fixed, so output is demand-determined).

Understanding these assumptions is essential for using the IS-LM model appropriately. The fixed price assumption means the model explains short-run fluctuations in output (business cycles), not long-run growth or inflation. The closed economy assumption simplifies the analysis but means the model does not apply to open economies (like Canada) without modification. The short-run framework means the model assumes that productive capacity is fixed, so changes in demand directly affect output (Keynesian assumption).

In this section, we will examine each assumption in detail: why fixed prices are a reasonable short-run approximation (sticky wages and prices), the implications of a closed economy (no exchange rate channel, no capital flows), and the meaning of the short run (output is demand-determined). We will also discuss the limitations of these assumptions and how the model can be extended (open economy IS-LM, AD-AS for price adjustments). You will learn to identify when the IS-LM model is appropriate, what it can explain, and what it cannot.

core_concept.principle:
The IS-LM model is built on three core assumptions that define its scope and applicability:

1. Fixed Prices (Sticky Prices/Sticky Wages):
• The price level ($P$) is assumed constant in the short run.
• This reflects sticky wages and sticky prices: wages and prices do not adjust immediately to changes in demand.
• Because prices are fixed, changes in nominal variables (money supply, interest rates) translate into changes in real variables (output, employment).
• Monetary policy is non-neutral in the short run (affects real output).
• This contrasts with the long-run classical model where prices are flexible and money is neutral.

2. Closed Economy (No International Trade or Capital Flows):
• The economy is assumed to have no trade with other countries ($NX = 0$).
• No imports, no exports, no capital flows, no exchange rate.
• Aggregate demand is composed only of consumption ($C$), investment ($I$), and government spending ($G$).
• This simplifies the model but means it does not directly apply to open economies like Canada.
• Extensions (Mundell-Fleming model) add international trade and capital mobility.

3. Short-Run Framework (Output is Demand-Determined):
• Capital stock ($K$) and technology ($A$) are fixed (no long-run growth).
• Labor is not necessarily fully employed; output is determined by aggregate demand, not supply capacity.
• This reflects the Keynesian view that recessions are caused by insufficient demand.
• The economy can be at less than full employment (output gap).
• In the long run, the model does not apply; the AD-AS model is used instead.

Key Implications of the Assumptions:
• Because prices are fixed, the nominal interest rate ($i$) and the real interest rate ($r$) are equal ($i = r$).
• The IS curve shows equilibrium in the goods market: $Y = C(Y - T) + I(i) + G$.
• The LM curve shows equilibrium in the money market: $M/P = L(Y, i)$.
• The intersection of IS and LM determines short-run equilibrium output and interest rate.
• Monetary policy works through the LM curve (shifting it via changes in $M$).
• Fiscal policy works through the IS curve (shifting it via changes in $G$ or $T$).

Limitations of the Assumptions:
• Fixed prices do not hold in the long run or during high inflation (1970s, 2020s).
• Closed economy assumption does not apply to small open economies (Canada) or globalized world.
• Short-run framework does not explain growth or long-run determinants of output.
• The model was criticized in the 1970s (stagflation) and largely replaced by AD-AS and New Keynesian models.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine you want to understand how a monetary policy shock (like a Fed rate cut) affects the economy in the short run. You cannot use the classical model (where prices adjust instantly) because that model predicts monetary neutrality: a rate cut would just cause inflation, not higher output. But in reality, rate cuts do boost output in the short run (6-12 months) before inflation responds. The IS-LM model captures this by assuming prices are fixed in the short run. With fixed prices, a rate cut lowers borrowing costs, stimulating investment and consumption, increasing output.

Now imagine you are analyzing the Canadian economy. Canada trades heavily with the US (exports = 30% of GDP). The basic IS-LM model assumes a closed economy (no trade), so it would miss the exchange rate channel and the impact of US demand on Canada. To analyze Canada, you would use the Mundell-Fleming model (open economy IS-LM), which relaxes the closed economy assumption.

The short-run framework means the model assumes that the capital stock and technology are fixed. Output is determined by how much people want to spend (aggregate demand), not by how much the economy can produce (potential output). This is reasonable for analyzing recessions (when there is slack) but not for analyzing long-run growth (when supply constraints bind).

The IS-LM model is a 'toy model'—simplified to teach intuition. Modern macroeconomists use more complex models (DSGE, New Keynesian), but the IS-LM framework remains the starting point for understanding how monetary and fiscal policy affect the economy in the short run.

image.alt: Diagram: Three assumptions (fixed prices, closed economy, short-run) with arrows to implications (monetary non-neutrality, no trade, demand-determined output).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.1: The three core assumptions of the IS-LM model define its scope and applicability.

sections.introduction:
The IS-LM model, developed by John Hicks (1937) and Alvin Hansen (1949), is a macroeconomic framework that shows the relationship between interest rates and real output in the goods market (IS curve) and the money market (LM curve). It was the dominant macroeconomic model from the 1950s through the 1970s and remains a foundational tool for understanding the interaction between real and financial sectors, the effects of monetary and fiscal policy, and the determination of short-run equilibrium output and interest rates.

The IS-LM model is built on several key assumptions that define its scope and applicability. These assumptions are critical for understanding what the model can and cannot explain, and for interpreting its predictions correctly. The three core assumptions are: (1) fixed prices (the price level is assumed constant in the short run), (2) a closed economy (no international trade or capital flows), and (3) a short-run framework (capital stock and technology are fixed, so output is demand-determined).

In this section, we will examine each assumption in detail: why fixed prices are a reasonable short-run approximation (sticky wages and prices), the implications of a closed economy (no exchange rate channel, no capital flows), and the meaning of the short run (output is demand-determined). We will also discuss the limitations of these assumptions and how the model can be extended (open economy IS-LM, AD-AS for price adjustments).

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Time horizon (short run, medium run, long run); IS-LM applies to short run (fixed prices, demand-determined output); AD-AS applies to medium run (prices adjust); Solow model applies to long run (growth).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 9.1.2: The IS-LM model applies to the short run (prices fixed, output demand-determined); other models are needed for medium-run price adjustment and long-run growth.

key_concept.definition:
IS-LM Model Assumptions: The three core assumptions that define the scope and applicability of the IS-LM framework.

1. Fixed Prices (Sticky Prices/Sticky Wages):
• The price level ($P$) is assumed constant in the short run.
• Rationale: Wages and prices are 'sticky' due to menu costs, labor contracts, and nominal rigidities.
• Implication: Changes in nominal variables (money supply, nominal interest rates) affect real variables (output, employment). Monetary policy is non-neutral in the short run.
• The nominal interest rate ($i$) equals the real interest rate ($r$) because expected inflation ($\pi^e$) is zero or constant ($i = r + \pi^e$, but with $\pi^e = 0$, $i = r$).
• This assumption breaks down in the long run (prices adjust) or during high inflation (prices adjust quickly).

2. Closed Economy (No International Trade or Capital Flows):
• The economy has no trade with other countries: exports ($X$) = 0, imports ($IM$) = 0, net exports ($NX$) = 0.
• No capital flows: no foreign borrowing or lending; no exchange rate.
• Aggregate demand: $AD = C + I + G$ (no $NX$ term).
• Rationale: Simplifies the model to focus on domestic interactions; reasonable for large economies (US) in the 1950s-1960s.
• Implication: The model cannot analyze exchange rate channels, trade balances, or spillovers from foreign economies.
• For open economies (Canada), the Mundell-Fleming model extends IS-LM by adding international trade and capital mobility.

3. Short-Run Framework (Output is Demand-Determined):
• Capital stock ($K$) and technology ($A$) are fixed (no long-run growth).
• Labor is not necessarily fully employed; output is determined by aggregate demand, not supply capacity.
• Rationale: In the short run, firms meet demand by adjusting labor hours (not capital). Recessions are caused by insufficient demand (Keynesian view).
• Implication: The economy can operate below potential (output gap). Monetary and fiscal policy can affect output in the short run.
• In the long run, supply-side factors (capital, labor, technology) determine output (potential GDP).

Additional Simplifying Assumptions (often made but not core):
• Investment depends only on the interest rate ($I = I(i)$, with $I'(i) < 0$).
• Consumption depends only on disposable income ($C = C(Y - T)$, with $0 < C' < 1$).
• Money demand depends on income and interest rate ($M_d = L(Y, i)$, with $L_Y > 0$, $L_i < 0$).
• Government spending ($G$) and taxes ($T$) are exogenous (policy variables).
• Money supply ($M$) is exogenous (controlled by central bank).
key_concept.context: The IS-LM model was developed during the Great Depression, when prices were falling (deflation) and output was far below potential. The assumption of fixed prices was a reasonable approximation for the 1930s (prices did not adjust quickly). The closed economy assumption reflected the lower level of globalization in the 1930s-1950s (trade as a share of GDP was much lower). The short-run framework reflected Keynes's focus on demand-determined output. The model was the workhorse of macroeconomics from the 1950s through the 1970s. It was criticized in the 1970s (stagflation: high inflation and high unemployment, which the model could not explain because it assumed fixed prices). Robert Lucas and other new classical economists argued that the assumptions were unrealistic and that models should have microfoundations. Today, the IS-LM model is still taught as a pedagogical tool, but modern macroeconomics uses New Keynesian DSGE models that incorporate microfoundations and price stickiness more rigorously.

image.alt: Photo of John Maynard Keynes and John Hicks; timeline: 1936 (General Theory), 1937 (Hicks IS-LM), 1949 (Hansen synthesis).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.3: The IS-LM model was developed by John Hicks (1937) based on Keynes's General Theory (1936) and popularized by Alvin Hansen (1949).

classifications.value: Prices are assumed constant in the short run; monetary policy affects real output (non-neutrality)
characteristics: Rationale: Sticky wages (labor contracts) and sticky prices (menu costs).
Nominal and real interest rates are equal ($i = r$) because expected inflation is zero or constant.
Monetary policy is non-neutral: changes in $M$ affect $Y$ in the short run.
In the long run, prices adjust and money becomes neutral (classical dichotomy).
Fixed price assumption fails during hyperinflation or when inflation is high (1970s, 2020s).
classifications.behavior:
The fixed price assumption is the defining feature of the Keynesian short run. In the classical model, prices adjust instantly to clear markets; a change in the money supply affects only prices, not output. In the IS-LM model, prices are fixed, so a change in the money supply affects output (through lower interest rates stimulating investment).

Why are prices sticky? Economists have proposed several explanations:
• Menu costs: Firms face costs of changing prices (printing new menus, updating systems).
• Labor contracts: Wages are set for 1-3 years; they cannot adjust instantly.
• Coordination failure: Firms do not change prices because they fear losing customers; if all firms changed prices together, they could, but coordination is difficult.
• Fairness concerns: Firms are reluctant to raise prices when demand increases because customers perceive it as unfair.

The fixed price assumption is most reasonable when inflation is low and stable (the 'Great Moderation' 1985-2007). It is less reasonable during high inflation (1970s, 2020s) when firms change prices frequently. During the 2021-2022 inflation surge, prices were not sticky; they adjusted rapidly. The IS-LM model would be less appropriate for analyzing that period; the AD-AS model (which allows price adjustment) would be better.

Despite its limitations, the fixed price assumption is useful for understanding how monetary policy affects the economy in the short run before prices have fully adjusted.

image.alt: Graph: Price level over time; sudden monetary shock; in short run (IS-LM) price fixed; in long run (AD-AS) price adjusts to new level.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.4: Fixed price assumption: in the short run (IS-LM), prices do not adjust to monetary shocks; in the long run (AD-AS), prices adjust and money becomes neutral.

examples.explanation: The Fed increases the money supply by 10%. Under fixed prices, the LM curve shifts right, lowering interest rates and increasing output. Real GDP rises by 2% in the short run. This is monetary non-neutrality.

The Fed increases the money supply by 10%. Under flexible prices, the price level rises by 10% immediately. Output and interest rates are unchanged. Money is neutral. The IS-LM model does not apply because the fixed price assumption is violated.

classifications.value: The economy has no international trade or capital flows; net exports are zero
characteristics: No capital flows: no foreign borrowing or lending.
No exchange rate ($E$) in the model.
Aggregate demand: $AD = C + I + G$ (no $NX$ term).
Reasonable for large economies (US) in the 1950s-1960s when trade was a smaller share of GDP.
Not reasonable for small open economies (Canada, Netherlands, Belgium) or for the modern globalized economy.
classifications.behavior:
The closed economy assumption greatly simplifies the IS-LM model by eliminating the exchange rate channel and foreign demand effects. In a closed economy, aggregate demand depends only on domestic consumption, investment, and government spending. There is no need to consider how changes in interest rates affect the exchange rate and net exports.

However, this assumption is unrealistic for most modern economies. In Canada, exports are 30% of GDP; the exchange rate channel is a major transmission mechanism for monetary policy. In the US, exports are 11% of GDP—still significant. Global trade has increased dramatically since the 1950s (from 20% of world GDP to 60%).

The Mundell-Fleming model (or IS-LM-BP model) relaxes the closed economy assumption by adding:
• Net exports: $NX = NX(E, Y, Y^*)$ (depends on exchange rate, domestic income, foreign income).
• Capital flows: $CF = CF(i - i^*)$ (depends on interest rate differential).
• Exchange rate: flexible or fixed.

For analyzing the Canadian economy, the Mundell-Fleming model is more appropriate than the closed-economy IS-LM. The Bank of Canada explicitly considers the exchange rate channel in its monetary policy framework.

Despite its limitations, the closed-economy IS-LM is still taught as a building block. Once you understand the closed-economy version, you can extend it to the open economy by adding $NX$ and exchange rates.

image.alt: Two diagrams: Closed economy (no trade, just C+I+G) vs. Open economy (C+I+G+NX with exports and imports).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.5: Closed economy assumption: the IS-LM model ignores international trade; the Mundell-Fleming model extends it to open economies.

examples.process: Closed Economy IS-LM for the US (1950s-1960s)
examples.explanation: In the 1950s, US trade was 5% of GDP. The closed economy assumption was a reasonable approximation. The IS-LM model successfully explained the effects of Fed policy and fiscal policy on output and interest rates.

examples.process: Why Closed Economy Fails for Canada
examples.explanation: Canada's exports are 30% of GDP, and 75% go to the US. A US recession reduces Canadian exports, shifting Canada's IS curve left. The closed-economy IS-LM cannot capture this spillover. The Mundell-Fleming model (open economy IS-LM) is needed to analyze Canada.

classifications.value: Capital stock and technology are fixed; output is determined by aggregate demand, not supply capacity
characteristics: Capital stock ($K$) and technology ($A$) are exogenous and fixed.
Labor is not necessarily fully employed; there can be involuntary unemployment.
Output is determined by aggregate demand: $Y = AD = C + I + G$ (in closed economy).
Firms meet demand by adjusting labor hours (not capital).
The economy can operate below potential (output gap).
In the long run, supply-side factors (capital, labor, technology) determine output (potential GDP).
classifications.behavior:
The short-run framework is the 'Keynesian' assumption that distinguishes the IS-LM model from classical models. In the classical model, output is always at potential (full employment) because prices adjust to clear markets. In the IS-LM model, output can be below potential due to insufficient demand. This is why recessions happen: a fall in investment or consumption reduces aggregate demand, and because prices are sticky, output falls rather than prices.

The short-run framework means the model is appropriate for analyzing business cycles (fluctuations around potential) but not for analyzing long-run growth (trend increases in potential). For long-run growth, economists use the Solow model or endogenous growth models.

The 'short run' in IS-LM is defined as the period over which prices are fixed and capital stock is fixed. This could be 6-18 months. Over longer horizons (2-5 years), prices adjust, and the AD-AS model is more appropriate. Over very long horizons (decades), the Solow model is used.

The demand-determined output assumption implies that the economy has slack (unemployed resources). If the economy is at full employment (output gap = 0), the IS-LM model still applies, but the multiplier effects are smaller because there is crowding out. At full employment, fiscal policy may just raise interest rates without increasing output (crowding out).

image.alt: Graph: Short-run (IS-LM) output determined by AD, can be below potential; long-run (classical) output at potential; AD-AS bridges the gap.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.6: Short-run vs. long-run: in the short run (IS-LM), output is demand-determined and can deviate from potential; in the long run, output returns to potential through price adjustment.

examples.explanation: During the 2008 crisis, investment collapsed. In the IS-LM model (short run, fixed prices), the IS curve shifted left, and output fell. The model explains why the recession occurred: insufficient demand, not a supply shock.

examples.process: Why Not Use IS-LM for Long-Run Growth
examples.explanation: From 1990 to 2020, US potential GDP grew at about 2% per year due to population growth, capital accumulation, and technological progress. The IS-LM model (fixed capital stock) cannot explain this growth. The Solow model is used instead.

principles.principle: The IS-LM Model Is a Short-Run Model; It Does Not Explain Inflation or Long-Run Growth
principles.statement: The IS-LM model assumes fixed prices and a fixed capital stock. This makes it appropriate for analyzing business cycles (short-run fluctuations in output and interest rates) but not for analyzing inflation (which requires price adjustment) or long-run growth (which requires capital accumulation and technological progress). The model's predictions about monetary neutrality (non-neutrality in the short run) do not apply to the long run. To analyze inflation, economists use the AD-AS model (which allows prices to adjust). To analyze long-run growth, economists use the Solow model or endogenous growth models. The IS-LM model is a 'toy model'—a simplified framework for teaching intuition—not a complete description of the economy. Modern macroeconomists use New Keynesian DSGE models that incorporate microfoundations, price stickiness, and growth dynamics, but the IS-LM intuition remains valuable.
principles.application: When using the IS-LM model, be explicit about the time horizon. The model is most useful for analyzing policy effects over a 6-18 month horizon. For longer horizons, add price adjustment (AD-AS). For questions about potential output or growth, use growth models. Do not use the IS-LM model to analyze hyperinflation (prices are not fixed) or technological change (capital stock is not fixed). The model is also less useful during periods of high inflation (1970s, 2020s) because the fixed price assumption is violated.
principles.example: In 2021-2022, inflation surged to 9%. The IS-LM model (with fixed prices) cannot explain this inflation because it assumes prices are constant. The AD-AS model (which allows prices to adjust) is needed. The AD-AS model shows that the pandemic shifted both AD (fiscal stimulus) and AS (supply chain disruptions), causing a large increase in prices. The IS-LM model would have missed the inflation entirely.

image.alt: Two panels: IS-LM (fixed prices, output adjustment) and AD-AS (price adjustment, output returns to potential).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.7: IS-LM vs. AD-AS: IS-LM assumes fixed prices (short run); AD-AS allows price adjustment (medium run). Both are needed for a complete analysis.

principles.principle: The Closed Economy Assumption is Unrealistic for Most Modern Economies, Especially Small Open Economies Like Canada
principles.statement: The closed economy assumption was reasonable when the IS-LM model was developed (1930s-1950s), when trade was a smaller share of GDP. Today, global trade is 60% of world GDP, and most economies are open. For small open economies like Canada (exports = 30% of GDP), the closed-economy IS-LM is highly misleading. It misses the exchange rate channel (monetary policy affects the currency, which affects net exports) and foreign demand spillovers (a US recession reduces Canadian exports). The Mundell-Fleming model (open economy IS-LM) extends the framework by adding net exports and capital flows. For large economies like the US (exports = 11% of GDP), the closed-economy assumption is a reasonable approximation, but even the US is affected by global conditions. Modern macroeconomics uses open economy models for policy analysis.
principles.application: If you are analyzing Canada, do not use the closed-economy IS-LM. Use the Mundell-Fleming model instead. If you are analyzing the US, the closed-economy IS-LM may be acceptable for some questions, but be aware of the limitations. When reading IS-LM analysis in textbooks, remember that the closed economy assumption is a simplification; real-world policy must consider international spillovers. The Bank of Canada explicitly models the exchange rate channel; the Fed also considers global conditions (e.g., dollar appreciation, foreign demand).
principles.example: In 2015, the BoC cut rates while the Fed was on hold. The closed-economy IS-LM would predict that the rate cut would lower interest rates and boost investment. In reality, the rate cut also depreciated the CAD (from 0.85 USD to 0.75 USD), boosting net exports. The open economy effect was as important as the domestic interest rate effect. The closed-economy model would have missed this.

image.alt: Diagram: Closed economy IS-LM (C+I+G) extended to open economy Mundell-Fleming (C+I+G+NX) with exchange rate channel.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.8: The open economy extension: adding net exports and exchange rates to the IS-LM model creates the Mundell-Fleming model, which is essential for analyzing Canada.

worked_examples.title: Identifying When the IS-LM Model Is Appropriate

image.alt: Table: Scenario (recession, high inflation, long-run growth, oil price shock, Canada monetary policy, US fiscal policy) and whether IS-LM applies.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.9: Worked example: identifying when the IS-LM model (with its assumptions) is appropriate for analysis.

worked_examples.problem:
For each scenario, determine whether the basic closed-economy IS-LM model (with fixed prices) is appropriate. If not, explain which assumption is violated and what model would be better.

a) The US economy enters a recession due to a fall in consumer confidence. The Fed is considering a rate cut. (Assume inflation is low, around 2%.)
b) Canada experiences a sudden depreciation of the CAD due to falling oil prices. The BoC is considering a rate cut.
c) The US experiences high inflation (8%) due to supply chain disruptions and excess demand. The Fed is considering rate hikes.
d) A country wants to analyze long-run growth (over 30 years) from capital accumulation and technological progress.
e) A small European country with a fixed exchange rate to the euro faces a recession. The central bank cannot cut rates (pegged currency).

step_by_step.step: Analyze scenario (a): US recession, low inflation
step_by_step.explanation:
Appropriate. Inflation is low, so the fixed price assumption is reasonable. The US is a large economy, so the closed economy assumption is a reasonable approximation. The time horizon is short run (recession, policy response). The IS-LM model would be appropriate for analyzing the effects of a Fed rate cut on output and interest rates.

However, even for the US, the open economy version (Mundell-Fleming) would be more accurate, but the closed-economy IS-LM is acceptable for pedagogical purposes.

Not appropriate (closed economy assumption violated). Canada is a small open economy with exports = 30% of GDP. The exchange rate channel is a major transmission mechanism. The closed-economy IS-LM would miss the effect of the CAD depreciation on net exports. The appropriate model is the Mundell-Fleming (open economy IS-LM) model, which includes net exports and the exchange rate.

Specifically, the BoC rate cut would further depreciate the CAD (if no other changes), boosting net exports. The closed-economy model would ignore this effect, potentially underestimating the impact of the rate cut.

Not appropriate (fixed price assumption violated). With inflation at 8%, prices are not fixed; they are changing rapidly. The IS-LM model assumes constant prices, so it cannot analyze inflation. The appropriate model is the AD-AS (Aggregate Demand-Aggregate Supply) model, which allows prices to adjust. The AD-AS model can show how demand and supply shocks affect both output and the price level.

The IS-LM model could still be used if you reinterpret it as a model of real variables with inflation expected, but the basic version assumes fixed prices. For high inflation periods, the fixed price assumption is unrealistic.

step_by_step.step: Analyze scenario (d): Long-run growth over 30 years
step_by_step.explanation:
Not appropriate (short-run framework violated). The IS-LM model assumes fixed capital stock and technology. Over 30 years, capital accumulates and technology improves, increasing potential output. The appropriate model is the Solow growth model (or endogenous growth models), which analyzes how saving, population growth, and technological progress affect long-run output per capita.

The IS-LM model is a short-run business cycle model; it cannot explain long-run growth. For long-run questions, use growth models, not IS-LM.

step_by_step.step: Analyze scenario (e): Small European country with fixed exchange rate
step_by_step.explanation:
Not appropriate (closed economy and flexible exchange rate assumptions violated). The country is small and open (closed economy assumption violated). It has a fixed exchange rate (IS-LM assumes flexible exchange rates or no exchange rate). Under a fixed exchange rate, monetary policy is not independent; the central bank must maintain the peg. The appropriate model is the Mundell-Fleming model with fixed exchange rates. This model shows that under fixed rates, monetary policy is ineffective (the central bank cannot cut rates because it would put downward pressure on the currency, forcing intervention). Fiscal policy becomes more effective.

The closed-economy IS-LM would incorrectly suggest that the central bank can cut rates to stimulate the economy. The open economy model with fixed rates shows that the rate cut would be impossible or would force a devaluation.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Appropriate (low inflation, US large economy). b) Not appropriate (open economy Canada; use Mundell-Fleming). c) Not appropriate (high inflation; use AD-AS). d) Not appropriate (long-run growth; use Solow model). e) Not appropriate (fixed exchange rate, small open economy; use Mundell-Fleming with fixed rates).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the three assumptions of the IS-LM model to determine when the model is appropriate for analyzing different economic scenarios.

worked_examples.title: Comparing IS-LM Predictions Under Fixed vs. Flexible Prices

image.alt: Two graphs: IS-LM (fixed prices, output increases after money supply increase) vs. Classical (flexible prices, only prices increase).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.10: Worked example: comparing the effects of a money supply increase under fixed prices (IS-LM) vs. flexible prices (classical model).

worked_examples.problem:
The central bank increases the money supply by 10%. Compare the effects under two different price assumptions:

Case A: Fixed Prices (IS-LM Assumption)
• Prices are constant ($P$ fixed).
• The economy has slack (output below potential).

Case B: Flexible Prices (Classical Assumption)
• Prices adjust instantly.
• The economy is at potential output ($Y = Y^*$).

a) In Case A (fixed prices), what happens to output ($Y$), the interest rate ($i$), and the price level ($P$)?
b) In Case B (flexible prices), what happens to output ($Y$), the interest rate ($i$), and the price level ($P$)?
c) Why does money affect output in Case A but not in Case B?
d) Which assumption (fixed or flexible prices) is more realistic in the short run (6-12 months)? Which is more realistic in the long run (5+ years)?
e) If the economy is at full employment (no slack) but prices are sticky, how would the effects differ from Case A?

step_by_step.explanation:
Money supply increase ($M \uparrow$) → LM curve shifts right → at original interest rate, excess supply of money → people buy bonds → bond prices rise, interest rate falls → lower interest rate stimulates investment ($I \uparrow$) → aggregate demand increases ($AD \uparrow$) → output increases ($Y \uparrow$) because firms meet demand by hiring more workers (prices fixed).

Results: $Y \uparrow$, $i \downarrow$, $P$ unchanged (fixed).

Money supply increase ($M \uparrow$) → at original prices, excess supply of money → people try to spend it → but output is already at potential ($Y = Y^*$), so spending drives up prices → price level rises ($P \uparrow$) → real money supply $M/P$ returns to original level → interest rate returns to original level (determined by real factors) → output unchanged at potential.

Results: $Y$ unchanged (at $Y^*$), $i$ unchanged, $P \uparrow$ (proportional to $M$ increase)

This is the classical dichotomy: money is neutral in the long run.

step_by_step.step: Explain why money affects output in Case A but not Case B
step_by_step.explanation: Money affects output in Case A (fixed prices) because prices do not adjust to absorb the money supply increase. Instead, the extra money lowers interest rates, stimulating investment and output. In Case B (flexible prices), prices adjust immediately, so the real money supply ($M/P$) does not change. Since real money supply is unchanged, interest rates do not change, and output remains at potential. The key is price stickiness: when prices are sticky, changes in nominal money supply become changes in real money supply, affecting interest rates and output. When prices are flexible, nominal changes translate directly into price changes, leaving real variables unchanged.

step_by_step.step: Discuss realism of each assumption over different horizons
step_by_step.explanation:
Short run (6-12 months): Fixed prices are more realistic. In the short run, many prices are sticky due to menu costs, labor contracts, and customer relationships. Empirical evidence shows that monetary policy affects output with a lag of 6-12 months, consistent with fixed prices. The IS-LM model captures this.

Long run (5+ years): Flexible prices are more realistic. Over longer horizons, prices adjust. A sustained increase in money supply leads to higher prices, not higher output. The classical dichotomy holds in the long run. The AD-AS model captures this transition.

The IS-LM model is appropriate for short-run analysis (business cycles). The classical model is appropriate for long-run analysis (inflation, growth). Modern macroeconomics synthesizes both: sticky prices in the short run, flexible prices in the long run.

step_by_step.step: Discuss full employment with sticky prices
step_by_step.explanation:
If the economy is at full employment ($Y = Y^*$) but prices are sticky, the effects of a money supply increase would be:
• In the very short run (before prices adjust), the LM curve shifts right, lowering interest rates. But since output cannot exceed potential (no slack), firms cannot increase output. Instead, there may be excess demand (shortages) as people try to spend more than the economy can produce. This excess demand puts upward pressure on prices. Over time, prices rise, shifting the LM curve back left until output returns to potential and interest rates return to original levels.

The difference from Case A (slack) is that output does not increase because the economy is already at capacity. The money supply increase causes inflation, not output growth, even in the short run. This is why monetary policy is less effective when the economy is at full employment—it mainly causes inflation.

In practice, the Fed considers the output gap: when the output gap is positive (overheating), rate hikes are needed to prevent inflation; when the output gap is negative (slack), rate cuts can boost output without causing inflation (up to a point).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Y ↑, i ↓, P unchanged. b) Y unchanged, i unchanged, P ↑ (proportional). c) Fixed prices: real M/P changes; flexible prices: only nominal changes. d) Short run: fixed prices more realistic; long run: flexible prices more realistic. e) With full employment, output cannot increase; excess demand causes inflation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the fixed vs. flexible price assumptions to compare the effects of monetary policy in the IS-LM model vs. the classical model.

process_flow.process: Specify the time horizon (short run vs. long run)
process_flow.description: Before using the IS-LM model, determine whether the time horizon is short run (prices fixed, capital fixed) or long run (prices flexible, capital variable).
process_flow.mechanism: Short run (6-18 months): use IS-LM. Medium run (2-5 years): use AD-AS. Long run (decades): use growth models.

image.alt: Decision tree: Time horizon → short run (IS-LM), medium run (AD-AS), long run (Solow).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.11: Step 1 - Specify the time horizon to determine whether the IS-LM model is appropriate.

process_flow.process: Check if prices are reasonably stable (low inflation)
process_flow.description: The fixed price assumption requires that inflation is low and stable. If inflation is high or volatile, the IS-LM model is not appropriate.
process_flow.mechanism: If inflation > 5% or volatile, use AD-AS instead. If inflation is near target (2%), the fixed price assumption is reasonable for short-run analysis.

image.alt: Decision tree: Inflation low? → Yes (use IS-LM); No (use AD-AS).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.12: Step 2 - Check inflation; the fixed price assumption is only reasonable when inflation is low and stable.

process_flow.process: Determine if the economy is closed or open
process_flow.description: The basic IS-LM assumes a closed economy. For open economies, use the Mundell-Fleming model (open economy IS-LM).
process_flow.mechanism: If the economy is small and open (Canada, UK, Eurozone) or trade is significant, use open economy model. If the economy is large (US) and trade is small, closed economy may be acceptable.

image.alt: Decision tree: Economy open? → Yes (use Mundell-Fleming); No (use closed IS-LM).
image.caption: Figure 9.1.13: Step 3 - Determine if the economy is open; for open economies, use the Mundell-Fleming extension.

process_flow.process: Assume output is demand-determined (Keynesian short run)
process_flow.description: Assume that firms meet demand by adjusting labor hours, not prices. Output can be below potential.
process_flow.mechanism: This assumption is reasonable during recessions (output gap negative) but less reasonable during booms (output gap positive). At full employment, the model's predictions are less reliable.

image.alt: Diagram: Aggregate demand determines output; firms meet demand; labor adjusts.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.14: Step 4 - Assume output is demand-determined (Keynesian short run) with slack in the economy.

process_flow.process: Apply the IS-LM model for short-run policy analysis
process_flow.description: Once the assumptions are satisfied, use the IS-LM model to analyze the effects of monetary and fiscal policy on output and interest rates.
process_flow.mechanism: IS curve: $Y = C(Y-T) + I(i) + G$. LM curve: $M/P = L(Y, i)$. Intersection gives equilibrium $Y$ and $i$. Changes in $G$, $T$, or $M$ shift curves.

image.alt: Diagram: IS and LM curves; policy shocks shift curves; new equilibrium output and interest rate.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.15: Step 5 - Apply the IS-LM model to analyze short-run policy effects under the three core assumptions.

image.alt: Master framework: Time horizon (short run) + price stability (low inflation) + closed economy (or open extension) + demand-determined output → IS-LM model applicable. Otherwise, use alternative models.
image.caption: Figure 9.1.16: Complete framework for applying the IS-LM model: check assumptions before using.

summary: The IS-LM model is built on three core assumptions that define its scope and applicability. First, fixed prices (sticky wages and sticky prices) mean that the price level is constant in the short run, so changes in nominal variables (money supply) affect real variables (output). This reflects the Keynesian view that monetary policy is non-neutral in the short run. Second, the closed economy assumption means there is no international trade or capital flows; aggregate demand consists only of consumption, investment, and government spending. This simplifies the model but makes it inappropriate for open economies like Canada (which require the Mundell-Fleming extension). Third, the short-run framework assumes that capital stock and technology are fixed, so output is demand-determined. The economy can operate below potential (output gap). This contrasts with long-run classical models where output is at potential. The IS-LM model is appropriate for analyzing short-run business cycles (6-18 months) when inflation is low and stable. It is not appropriate for analyzing high inflation (use AD-AS), long-run growth (use Solow), or open economies without modification (use Mundell-Fleming). Understanding these assumptions is essential for using the model correctly and interpreting its predictions.
